text.title: Product Development Trends 2026: Rules and Agent Gotchas
text.summary: Source-freshness rules, survivorship bias guards, greenwashing prevention, duplicate-doc confusion handling, and AI-agent gotchas for the trend-scanning workflow.

rule.statement: Pin every trend bullet to ≥2 dated sources — at least one primary (Stack Overflow Survey, State of DevOps, GitHub Octoverse, Gartner) and one secondary (analyst commentary, Lenny's Newsletter). Reject any bullet with fewer than two dated sources.
rule.rationale: Single-source trend claims are indistinguishable from one viral blog post that may not reflect broad adoption. Two independent sources pointing in the same direction provide meaningfully higher confidence.

rule.statement: Validate publication dates with curl -I against the URL and reject 404s. Agents fabricate 2026-dated articles from publishers that have not published them.
rule.rationale: Source-date hallucination is the most common failure in trend research. A URL that returns 404 or a date mismatch catches the fabrication before it propagates into deliverables.

rule.statement: When citing "days not weeks" speed claims, name the benchmark cohort (e.g., "DORA elite performers, top 25%"). Do not quote elite-performer data as if it is the median.
rule.rationale: DORA data shows elite performers; median enterprise still ships in months. Quoting the elite as median systematically miscalibrates expectations in planning and positioning.

rule.statement: Cap consumer-trend bullets at 5. Require a third-party certification or measurable behaviour change per sustainability/ESG claim, not just a survey response.
rule.rationale: Agents balloon consumer-trend sections to 20 vague items without evidence. "Brands that do good" is a ten-year recycled talking point — require a 2025-or-later primary source or drop it.

rule.statement: When loading trend methodology content, require an explicit slug in the prompt (product-development-trends-2026 vs product-development-trends). Never load both without explicitly deduplicating.
rule.rationale: Two near-identical folders exist. Agents asked to "load latest trends" may load the older one; the explicit slug prevents the silent loading of stale content.

rule.statement: Before another research round, resolve the two folders (product-development-trends/ and product-development-trends-2026/) — pick one canonical path and archive the other.
rule.rationale: Duplicate content creates routing confusion for agents and version drift between the two files. One canonical path eliminates both failure modes.

antipattern.description: Confirmation echo: asked "is X still a trend in 2026?", agent answers yes by default without sourcing.
antipattern.reason: Force the rubric — state ∈ 4 values with citations, no narrative answer. "Yes" is not a valid rubric output.

antipattern.description: Buzzword bloat: "AI-augmented", "data-driven", "cross-functional" expand into paragraphs of nothing.
antipattern.reason: Cap each bucket's prose at ≤80 words in the snapshot. Length is inversely correlated with evidence density in trend writing.

antipattern.description: Vendor-source bias: McKinsey/BCG/a16z articles promote their portfolio narrative.
antipattern.reason: Require ≥1 community/OSS source (Stack Overflow, GitHub Octoverse) per bucket to balance the vendor-interest angle.

antipattern.description: "Cross-functional teams" treated as a recipe rather than an org change.
antipattern.reason: Cross-functional teams require structural org changes (reporting lines, incentives, co-location) — treating it as a methodology step produces vacuous prescriptions that agents cannot execute.
